locked_facts: Total momentum conserved when no external force: p_before = p_after
KE conserved in ELASTIC collision only
In perfectly inelastic collision: objects stick together, v = (m₁v₁+m₂v₂)/(m₁+m₂)
Momentum is a vector: direction matters
Center of mass velocity unchanged during collision
Coefficient of restitution e = 1 (elastic), e = 0 (perfectly inelastic)
truth_statements: The total momentum of an isolated system is conserved
In an elastic collision, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved
In a perfectly inelastic collision, the objects stick together and momentum is conserved but KE is not
Momentum conservation applies to the system, not individual objects
The velocity of the centre of mass remains unchanged in a collision with no external forces

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two collisions side by side — elastic (top) and perfectly inelastic (bottom). Momentum arrows before and after are IDENTICAL in both cases. But KE bars differ: KE is conserved in elastic, KE drops in inelastic.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Momentum arrows exactly equal before and after in BOTH collision types — but KE bar drops only in the inelastic case
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that momentum conservation does NOT mean speeds stay the same — momentum is conserved but KE may not be

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Two objects approaching each other with velocity arrows and momentum arrows (p = mv) labelled. Total momentum bar shown at top.
simulation_states.emphasis: Total momentum = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ calculated and displayed before collision

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Elastic collision occurs. Objects bounce apart with new velocities. Post-collision momentum arrows shown. Total momentum bar unchanged.
simulation_states.emphasis: After elastic collision: individual momenta change but total momentum is exactly the same as before

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Split view: elastic collision (top) and perfectly inelastic collision (bottom) with the same initial conditions. Momentum arrows identical in both. KE bars: conserved (top), reduced (bottom).
simulation_states.emphasis: Momentum is conserved in BOTH cases — but KE is only conserved in elastic. Same momentum, different speeds.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust masses of both objects. See elastic vs inelastic outcomes. Toggle between collision types. Momentum bars always match before and after.
simulation_states.emphasis: Try equal masses: in elastic collision, objects exchange velocities. Try very different masses: heavy object barely changes speed.

common_misconceptions.belief: Momentum conservation means objects bounce back with the same speed
trigger_phrases: same speed se bounce hota hai
collision mein speed same rehti hai
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT says total momentum of the system is conserved, not individual speeds. In inelastic collisions objects stick together and move with a common velocity different from both initial speeds.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show elastic vs inelastic collision side by side — momentum arrows identical but speeds clearly different in inelastic case

common_traps: confusing momentum conservation with KE conservation
using wrong sign for velocities in opposite directions
